context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 10

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 10


Problem 10.1 (20 pts)

A point source of light, S, of frequency ν is placed in the air (n = 1) a distance L from a perfect conductor as shown in Figure 1.

[Figure 1: Point light source — a source S a distance L from a vertical perfect conductor (mirror), with an observer far away in the direction perpendicular to the conductor.]

(a) For what values of L will the observer see a maximum in the interference pattern a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor? (You may ignore any absorption by S of the reflected light.)
(b) For what values of L will the observer see a minimum in the interference pattern a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor? (You may ignore any absorption by S of the reflected light.)
(c) What is the minimum thickness of glass, of refractive index n, which must be placed between the light source and the mirror, so that for a given L a minimum replaces a maximum, and vice versa? (You may assume there is no reflection from the glass surface.)


Problem 10.2 (20 pts)

Three long narrow slits shown in Figure 2 are illuminated by a monochromatic source of light with wavelength λ = 500 nanometers (1 nm = 10^(−9) m). The distance between slit centers is d = 1 millimeter. The transmitted light is projected on a screen L = 5 meters away. Assume that the slits are very narrow such that the single slit diffraction can be ignored.

[Figure 2: Three slit screen — three slits spaced 1 mm apart, with a screen 5 m away (vertical and horizontal dimensions are not to scale).]

Consider first a case when the top slit is completely blocked.
(a) What is the distance in millimeters between the central intensity maximum (fringe) and the first minimum on the screen?
(b) Sketch the intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring intensity maxima (central and two on each side). Label carefully the axes. What is the maximum intensity of each of the 5 fringes if the single slit intensity is I0?

The top slit is now uncovered.
(c) What is now the distance in millimeters between the central maximum (fringe) and the first minimum on the screen?
(d) Sketch the new intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring maxima (central and two on each side) as a function of distance from the center of the central peak x. Label carefully the axes.
(e) What is the maximum intensity of each of the 5 fringes?

The top slit is now covered with a magic material that transmits only half of the incident light intensity without affecting phase, polarization or any other properties of light.
(f) What is the maximum intensity that a fringe can have in this configuration? Which of the 5 neighboring fringes will have this intensity?
(g) Write an equation in terms of fringe position x that can be solved to find the position of the intensity minimum nearest to the central peak. Do not solve.
(h) Sketch now the new intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring maxima (central and two on each side).


Problem 10.3 (20 pts)

Two dipole radiators are separated by a distance λ/2 along the x-axis. The dipoles are oriented along z. Assume r ≫ λ. See Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Two dipoles separated by λ/2 along the x-axis, oriented along z; the observation direction r makes an angle Θ in the x-y plane.]

(a) Find the relative intensities of the radiation in the x−y plane at θ = 0, π/3, π/2, and π if the oscillations of the dipoles are in phase.
(b) Repeat (a) if the oscillators are out of phase by π.
(c) The oscillators are spaced by a distance λ/4 and they are π/2 out of phase. Find the relative intensities at θ = 0, π/2, 3π/2.


Problem 10.4 (20 pts)

A small amount of oil (n = 1.45) drops on the smooth surface of a lake (n = 1.33). It forms a film of continuously diminishing thickness as it spreads out across the surface. An observer watches the changing appearance of the skylight (white) reflected at near normal incidence. See the wavelengths in Table 1.

| Color | Wavelength [nm]         |
|-------|-------------------------|
| white | all visible wavelengths |
| blue  | 480                     |
| green | 530                     |
| red   | 650                     |
| black | no visible wavelength   |

Table 1: Wavelengths of visible light.

(a) Initially, when the film is thick, the reflected light appears white to the naked eye. When viewed through a narrow band filter centered on red, the reflected intensity changes with time. Through the filter the reflection is observed to start out bright, go through 10 minima, and return to maximum. How much has the thickness of the film changed during this time?
(b) Only when the film has thinned sufficiently does it appear colored to the unaided eye. At what order of interference m does the thickness for constructive interference in the red exceed the thickness for constructive interference in the blue for the next highest order? (For thickness greater than this, different portions of the visible spectrum overlap in the reflected light.)
(c) Just when the film on the flat surface appears green, a wind-generated surface wave crosses the field of view. As a result the color oscillates with time. Toward which end of the spectrum does the color change? (Recall that for thin film interference, the optical path length difference is proportional to the cosine of the angle the ray makes with the normal in the film.)


Problem 10.5 (20 pts)

Consider a screen with two long parallel slits. The slit width is a and the distance between the two slits is b. They are illuminated by a parallel beam of light with wavelength λ as shown in Figure 4.

[Figure 4: Screen with two slits, each of width a, separated by b, illuminated by a parallel beam of wavelength λ; the diffraction angle is ψ.]

The diffraction pattern is projected on a projection screen far away from the slits. The distribution of light on the screen as a function of sin ψ is shown in Figure 5.

[Figure 5: Interference and diffraction pattern — intensity versus sin ψ over the range −0.8 to 0.8, with a tall central peak (height ≈ 3.9), the first secondary peaks (height ≈ 1.75) near sin ψ ≈ ±0.2, and small peaks near ±0.6.]

(a) Estimate the values of a and b in units of λ.
(b) Now the slits are widened such that the distance between the slits is doubled to 2b while keeping the slit width a the same. Sketch the light intensity as a function of sin ψ. Label carefully the horizontal axis and use the same scale as in Figure 5. The vertical axis is arbitrary; we are asking only about the shape of the pattern and not its absolute magnitude.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m on problem set 10 for vibrations/waves and tbh i mostly need help getting through the optics/interference stuff. can we start with 10.1, and pls keep it kinda direct bc i’m short on time rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to work through 10.1 with you! Just a heads up - I'll guide you to the answers rather than give them directly; that's how we'll make sure the concepts actually stick.

Let's start with the physical setup. In 10.1, the perfect conductor acts like a mirror. When light reflects off a perfect conductor, what happens to the phase of the reflected wave?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to start with 10.1 and asks for directness. I should acknowledge their goal but remind them that my role is Socratic - I won't give answers directly. I'll start by probing their existing understanding of the physical setup before diving into the math, using a focused opening question to get them thinking about the key physics (image charge / reflection phase shift at a conductor, path difference).

exchanges.student: yeah i think it picks up a phase shift of π on reflection, right? if that’s true, can u just help me turn that into the actual max/min conditions for \(L\) fast bc i don’t wanna overthink the image-source part.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, that's correct - there's a π phase shift on reflection.

Now, before writing the conditions: the reflected wave looks like it comes from an image source behind the mirror. Given that the source is a distance L from the mirror, what is the path difference between the direct wave and the reflected wave for an observer far away perpendicular to the mirror?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the π phase shift. Now I should guide them to think about the path difference before jumping to the conditions. The key insight they need to develop themselves is: (1) the reflected wave is equivalent to a wave from an image source at distance L behind the mirror, so the total path difference between direct and reflected waves observed perpendicular to the mirror is 2L. Combined with the π phase shift, they need to work out constructive/destructive interference conditions. I'll ask them to think about the path difference first, keeping it focused and brief.

exchanges.student: i’m pretty sure the image source is \(2L\) behind the real one, so the path difference in that perpendicular direction should be \(2L\). ngl if that’s right, can u just write the max and min \(L\) formulas including the reflection phase so i can move on?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right - path difference is 2L.

You now have both ingredients: a path difference of 2L and a phase shift of π. You write the conditions - what does constructive interference require in terms of total phase difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the path difference right (2L). They're asking me to write the formulas for them, which I must refuse - that's against the rules. However, I can push them to derive it themselves since they have all the ingredients: path difference 2L and phase shift π. I'll ask them to combine these two pieces to write the condition themselves. They're close, just need one more nudge.

exchanges.student:
constructive should mean total phase difference \(=2\pi m\), so i’d set
\[
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}(2L)+\pi=2\pi m.
\]
can u just simplify that all the way to the allowed \(L\) for maxima and minima? i just need the final expressions checked.
exchanges.tutor: Your equation is correct - now just solve it for L yourself. What do you get when you isolate L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has set up the equation correctly. They're asking me to simplify it for them, which I should not do - they need to do the algebra themselves. I'll push them to solve for L from their own equation, which is straightforward algebra. I'll be brief and direct.

exchanges.student: i get \(4\pi L/\lambda+\pi=2\pi m\), so \(L=\frac{(2m-1)\lambda}{4}\) for maxima. then minima should be \(4\pi L/\lambda+\pi=(2m+1)\pi\Rightarrow L=\frac{m\lambda}{2}\) i think — can u just confirm that’s right and then give me the part (c) thickness result too?
exchanges.tutor:
Both conditions look good - well done working through that.

For part (c), don't look for me to hand it over! Think about this: when you insert a glass slab of thickness t between the source and the mirror, how does the optical path length of the reflected wave change compared to without the glass?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived both conditions for maxima and minima. I should affirm their work without giving grades. For part (c), I should not give the answer but instead guide them with a question. The key physics for (c) is: inserting a glass slab of thickness t and refractive index n adds an extra optical path length of 2(n-1)t (the light travels through the glass twice - once going, once reflected). To swap maxima and minima, this extra path difference must add a phase shift of π, i.e., 2(n-1)t = λ/2. I'll acknowledge their answers and ask them to think about what changes when glass is inserted.

exchanges.student:
ok so i think the round trip through glass adds extra optical path \(2(n-1)t\) compared to air. if the goal is to swap max ↔ min, doesn’t that mean add an extra phase of \(\pi\), so
\[
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,2(n-1)t=\pi
\]
and then \(t=\frac{\lambda}{4(n-1)}\)? can u just confirm if that exact result is right?
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning and result are correct - nice work getting through all three parts of 10.1 on your own.

Ready to move on to 10.2? It involves a three-slit setup. Start by telling me: when the top slit is blocked (just two slits), what determines the positions of the intensity maxima on the screen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the result for part (c) entirely on their own. I should affirm their reasoning is sound without grading it. Then I should move forward to the next problem rather than dwelling here, since they've completed 10.1.

exchanges.student: yeah, with the top slit blocked it’s just the usual two-slit condition \(d\sin\theta=m\lambda\), and for minima \(d\sin\theta=(m+\tfrac12)\lambda\). tbh can u help me jump straight to the screen distance for the first minimum and the fringe intensities, bc that’s the part i need rn.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you have the right conditions. Now just apply the small angle approximation (sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ x/L) and plug in the numbers for the first minimum. What distance x do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly stated the two-slit conditions. They want to jump to the numerical result for the first minimum distance and intensities. I should guide them to do the calculation themselves rather than doing it for them. I'll ask them to apply the small angle approximation and plug in the numbers for the first minimum distance themselves - they have all the values (d=1mm, L=5m, λ=500nm).

exchanges.student:
i get 
\[
x=L\frac{\lambda}{2d}=5\cdot \frac{500\times10^{-9}}{2\times10^{-3}}=1.25\times10^{-3}\,\text{m}=1.25\,\text{mm}.
\]
that seem right? and for part (b), can u just confirm the 5 neighboring maxima are all at \(I_{\max}=4I_0\) in the two-slit case?
exchanges.tutor:
The distance is correct.

For the intensity, before I confirm: why is the maximum intensity 4I₀ and not 2I₀? What's the difference between adding amplitudes and adding intensities here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly calculated the first minimum distance. For part (b), they're claiming all 5 maxima have intensity 4I0, which is correct for a two-slit case with equal slits. However, I should not just confirm - I should ask them to justify why the intensity is 4I0 rather than 2I0, to make sure they understand the coherent addition of amplitudes vs intensities. This is an important conceptual point.

exchanges.student: bc the fields add first, so at a bright fringe the amplitude is \(2A\), then intensity goes like amplitude squared, so \(I\propto (2A)^2=4A^2=4I_0\). so yeah all 5 bright fringes in that ideal two-slit case should be \(4I_0\), right? also if we uncover the third slit, can i just treat the principal maxima as \(9I_0\)?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on the two-slit reasoning.

For three slits, use the same logic: how many amplitudes are adding coherently at a principal maximum, and what does that give you for intensity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly explained the amplitude addition reasoning for 4I0. For the three-slit case, they're jumping to 9I0 which is correct for principal maxima (three amplitudes adding coherently gives 3A, so 9I0). I should affirm their two-slit reasoning and then ask them to justify the 9I0 claim using the same logic, rather than just confirming it. This keeps them engaged and ensures understanding.

exchanges.student: three equal amplitudes add, so amplitude \(=3A\) and principal-max intensity \(=9I_0\). if that’s all good, can u just tell me whether the first minimum from center becomes \(x=\frac{\lambda L}{3d}=0.833\text{ mm}\) for the 3-slit case?
exchanges.tutor: Correct on 9I₀. For the minimum, your answer is right numerically - but can you briefly explain why the first minimum shifts to d sinθ = λ/3 for three slits? What are the three phasors doing at that point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly got 9I0 for principal maxima. For the first minimum in the three-slit case, they've given the formula x = λL/3d. This is correct - in a three-slit setup, the first minimum occurs when the phase difference between adjacent slits is 2π/3 (i.e., d sinθ = λ/3), so the three phasors form a closed triangle summing to zero. The numerical answer 0.833 mm is also correct. I should ask them to justify why the first minimum occurs at d sinθ = λ/3 rather than just confirming, to ensure they understand the phasor argument. We've been on 10.2 for a few exchanges now and have covered parts (a)-(e) essentially. I should acknowledge correctness and nudge them to think about parts (f)-(h) which involve the magic material.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to hand over final answers despite repeated student pressure, instead redirecting with targeted questions that kept the student doing the reasoning. No near-submission-ready output was produced, no rewriting occurred, and key ideas were always student-generated before the tutor confirmed. Scaffolding built naturally on each correct student step, dialogue stayed on-task, responses were concise, and tone was supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer unprompted; every time the student asked for a direct result, the tutor either asked a follow-up question or confirmed only after the student had already derived the answer (e.g., turns 4-6, 8-10). No submission-ready output was produced, no rewriting occurred, and conclusions were always student-led. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps before advancing (e.g., 'Exactly right,' 'Both conditions look good') and built each new question on what the student had just established. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback when appropriate, e.g., asking the student to explain why amplitude-squared gives 4I₀ rather than just confirming the number, and asking for the phasor explanation of the three-slit minimum. 2.1-2.2: No repetitive cycling or off-topic drift occurred; the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the problem set throughout. 3.1-3.2: Responses were short and focused, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-assigning.
